hero.name: Aarav
hero.introduction: I am passionate about mathematics, coding, public speaking, and scientific innovation. Through GEMMA Clubs, I continuously develop my skills and participate in national and international competitions.

about.title: About Me
about.subtitle: A curious, driven student who believes that learning never stops — inside or outside the classroom.
paragraphs: Growing up with a deep fascination for numbers and technology, I have always sought challenges that push my thinking forward. Whether it's solving complex math problems, building a Scratch project, or presenting ideas on stage, I approach everything with enthusiasm and dedication.
My dream is to become an Aerospace Engineer — someone who contributes to humanity's journey beyond Earth. Every competition, project, and club activity brings me one step closer to that vision.

posts.title: How Abacus Improved My Mental Math Skills
posts.excerpt: When I first joined the Abacus Club in Grade 6, I could barely multiply two-digit numbers in my head. Two years later, I can solve 3-digit additions in under 2 seconds...
posts.content:
When I first joined the Abacus Club in Grade 6, I could barely multiply two-digit numbers in my head. Two years later, I can solve 3-digit additions in under 2 seconds. The journey from beginner to State-level finalist taught me one thing above all else: the brain is trainable.

Abacus isn't just a counting tool — it's a mental framework. You learn to visualize beads on a mental board, which activates spatial and numerical reasoning simultaneously. Research shows that consistent abacus practice increases the brain's grey matter in areas responsible for numerical processing.

My biggest breakthrough came during the 2023 State Championship. I had been practicing 45 minutes daily for three months. When the problems appeared on screen, my hands moved instinctively — but more importantly, my mental image of the abacus was crystal clear. I finished 20 seconds before the next competitor.

If you want to improve your math speed, I recommend starting with simple abacus exercises: just 15 minutes a day, consistent, for 30 days. The compound effect is remarkable. Your brain genuinely rewires itself.

posts.title: My Experience at the International Student Speaker Competition
posts.excerpt: Standing on stage in front of 200 people from 18 different countries, I realized how much the Public Speaking Club had transformed me. My legs were shaking, but my voice was steady...
posts.content:
Standing on stage in front of 200 people from 18 different countries, I realized how much the Public Speaking Club had transformed me. My legs were shaking, but my voice was steady.

The competition was held in Singapore. Each participant had 5 minutes to speak on a topic drawn randomly 30 minutes prior. My topic: "Technology is making us less human." I had to argue — and believe — one side within half an hour.

I chose to argue against the statement. My argument: technology, when used intentionally, amplifies our most human qualities — empathy, creativity, and connection. I referenced the use of social media in disaster relief, AI in medical diagnosis, and coding clubs that bring kids from different backgrounds together. Like ours.

I won the Bronze Medal. But more importantly, I left Singapore with four new friends from Korea, Brazil, and Kenya. We still talk. That's technology making us more human, not less.

To every student who thinks they can't speak publicly: you absolutely can. Join a club. Practice in front of a mirror. Start with two minutes. Grow from there. The stage is waiting for you.

posts.title: Why Coding is the Most Important Skill for Future Careers
posts.excerpt: Every industry is transforming. Healthcare uses AI. Banks run on algorithms. Farms use IoT sensors. Learning to code isn't just about becoming a programmer — it's about understanding the language of the future...
posts.content:
Every industry is transforming. Healthcare uses AI. Banks run on algorithms. Farms use IoT sensors. Learning to code isn't just about becoming a programmer — it's about understanding the language the future is written in.

I started coding in Scratch, which might sound basic. But Scratch taught me the fundamentals of computational thinking: breaking a problem into smaller pieces, designing loops, handling conditions, debugging when things go wrong. These are cognitive skills that apply everywhere — from writing a persuasive essay to engineering a satellite.

When I built my Climate Change Awareness website in HTML and CSS, I wasn't just learning syntax. I was learning to communicate: how to structure information, choose typography, create visual hierarchy, and guide a reader through an argument. The skills of a developer overlap completely with the skills of a writer, designer, and analyst.

My advice to anyone starting out: don't wait until you're "ready." Open Scratch today. Build something ugly and broken. Fix it. Build something better. The only way to learn coding is by coding.

In ten years, the students who learned to code today will be the ones shaping the world everyone else lives in. I'd rather be a shaper than a bystander.

list.text: Aarav consistently demonstrates curiosity, discipline, and leadership in every activity he participates in. His ability to grasp complex concepts quickly and then explain them to others sets him apart.
list.name: Mrs. Meera Sharma

list.text: The GEMMA Clubs have significantly improved Aarav's confidence and problem-solving abilities. We've watched him grow from a shy child into a poised young leader who takes initiative.
list.name: Rajan Sharma

list.text: As his club mentor, watching Aarav go from participant to mentor himself has been remarkable. He brings fresh ideas and genuine enthusiasm that motivates everyone around him.
list.name: Mr. Prashant Kumar
